title: Haemorrhoids
oneLiner: Symptomatic anal cushions causing bleeding, prolapse, and discomfort; common in adults, often misdiagnosed as cancer or fissure.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 38-year-old otherwise healthy woman presents with bright red blood on the toilet paper after defecation. She describes occasional anal itching and a sensation of incomplete emptying. She strains to pass hard stools. On direct questioning, she admits to prolonged sitting at work and a low-fibre diet. She is embarrassed and worried it might be cancer. Vital signs are normal. External inspection shows no lesion; digital rectal exam reveals soft, non-tender internal cushions that bulge on straining.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Bright red blood on paper** → low rectal/anal source; dark or mixed blood suggests proximal lesion.
- **Occasional itching** → mucous discharge from prolapsing cushions irritating perianal skin.
- **Sensation of incomplete emptying** → prolapsed haemorrhoids may mimic faecal mass (tenesmus).
- **Straining & hard stools** → exacerbating factors: chronic constipation increases intra-abdominal pressure.
- **Embarrassment and cancer fear** → typical patient delay; need careful reassurance + definitive exam.
- **Normal vitals** → no acute bleeding compromise; anaemia unlikely unless chronic heavy loss.
- **Soft internal cushions on DRE** → internal haemorrhoids above dentate line (insensate) – no pain on palpation.
- **Bulging on straining** → loss of supporting connective tissue; internal haemorrhoids may prolapse with Valsalva.

**Atypical presentations (that may trick you):**
- **Thrombosed external haemorrhoid:** acute severe perianal pain, feels a grape-like lump. The patient may think it's an abscess. No bright red blood, but may have dark discoloration. Urgent.
- **Strangulated internal haemorrhoid:** irreducible prolapse with purple-black discoloration, severe constant pain, possible necrosis. The patient may be febrile and tachycardic – mimics perianal sepsis.
- **Chronic anaemia without visible bleeding:** multiple low-volume bleeds over months. Patient presents with fatigue, pallor, low ferritin. Need to exclude proximal source before attributing to piles – especially if >50 years.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Haemorrhoids are pathological enlargement and distal displacement (prolapse) of the normal anal vascular cushions – not varicose veins. These cushions are composed of arteriovenous sinusoids, smooth muscle (Treitz's muscle), and connective tissue that normally aid fine continence. Disease occurs when the fibromuscular supporting frame degenerates, causing the cushions to slide down the anal canal. Symptoms depend on position: **internal** (above dentate line – insensate, cause painless bleeding/prolapse) vs **external** (below dentate line – sensate, cause pain only if thrombosed).

**Why it matters:**
- **Extremely common:** prevalence ~4–10% in adults; lifetime risk ~50%. Most self-manage or present late.
- **High mimicry profile:** bleeding haemorrhoids must be distinguished from colorectal cancer, inflammatory bowel disease, and anal fissure – especially in patients >45 years or with alarm features.
- **Common pitfalls:** missing external thrombus (patient in agony, misdiagnosed as abscess); assuming all rectal bleeding is 'just piles' (delays cancer diagnosis); over-prescribing topical steroids (skin atrophy, masking infection); under-treating internal prolapse (progression to strangulation).
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of development:**
Anatomy: three main vascular cushions (left lateral, right anterior, right posterior) lie in the submucosa of the anal canal. They are anchored by smooth muscle fibres (Treitz's muscle) and connective tissue that tether them to the internal sphincter and submucosa. This scaffold normally prevents displacement during defecation.

**Triggering events:**
Chronic straining (constipation, prolonged sitting, pregnancy) increases intra-abdominal pressure and shears the anchoring fibres. Over time, the cushions descend axially into the anal canal (Grade I: no prolapse, II: prolapse with straining reducible spontaneously, III: prolapse require manual reduction, IV: permanently prolapsed). As they descend, the overlying mucosa becomes thin and congested. The sinusoids within the cushion become engorged due to loss of external support and subsequent venous stasis.

**How signs arise:**

| Pathophysiologic step | Clinical manifestation | Why / Mechanism |
|----------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Mucosal thinning + faecal abrasion | **Bright red bleeding** | Thin mucosa over engorged sinusoids tears during passage of stool (splatter, on paper, or drips). Bleeding is arterial-sourced (hence bright) because cushions are fed by superior rectal artery branches. |
| Mucous discharge from prolapsed mucosa | **Perianal itching / moisture** | Mucosa secretes mucus; constant wetness causes maceration, irritant dermatitis. |
| Distal displacement of cushions | **Palpable 'lump' on straining** | Loss of fixation allows cushions to slide into the anal canal or externalise. |
| Thrombosis of external haemorrhoid | **Acute painful blue lump** | Straining causes rupture of a vein (subcutaneous haematoma) or in situ thrombosis within an external haemorrhoid. Pain due to stretch of overlying sensate skin (innervated by inferior rectal nerve). |
| Strangulation of prolapsed internal | **Severe constant pain + purple necrosis** | Sphincter spasm traps the prolapsed cushion, impairing venous return → congestion → arterial occlusion → infarction. Pain is severe because ischaemia now affects somatic nerve fibres in the anal verge. |

**Connecting to pattern cues:**
- *Painless bleeding* → internal haemorrhoids above dentate line (visceral afferents only – no pain).
- *Painful lump* → thrombosed external (somatic nerve supply) or strangulated internal.
- *Itching* → mucous seepage. Also consider skin tags (scar from resolved thrombus).
- *Sensation of incomplete emptying* → prolapsing mass creates false rectal fullness.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Specific cause / trigger | Stem clue that points to it |
|----------|--------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Idiopathic (most common)** | Degeneration of supporting connective tissue with age | Patient >40, no other risk factors; typical chronic presentation |
| **Mechanical / lifestyle** | Chronic constipation, straining, prolonged sitting, low-fibre diet | "I push hard to pass small pellets of stool"; office worker; "I eat on the run" |
| **Pregnancy / childbirth** | Increased intra-abdominal pressure + hormonal softening of pelvic ligaments | Complaints started in third trimester or early postpartum |
| **Portal hypertension** | Retrograde flow through portosystemic anastomoses → anorectal varices (not true haemorrhoids but present similarly) | Cirrhosis, ascites, oesophageal varices, splenomegaly; bleeding can be massive |
| **High venous pressure states** | Obesity, chronic cough (COPD), heavy lifting | "I lift weights at the gym" or "I have a smoker's cough" |
| **Malignancy (rare as cause)** | Rectal carcinoma obstructing drainage → secondary haemorrhoids | Unilateral, fixed, hard, or bleeding with altered bowel habit; age >50 |
| **Inflammatory bowel disease** | Crohn's disease (fistulating) or ulcerative colitis | Diarrhoea, abdominal pain, weight loss, extra-intestinal signs |

**Most common cause:** idiopathic degeneration + chronic constipation. Always ask about bowel habit and fibre intake.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnosis is clinical** – no lab test confirms haemorrhoids. Imaging is rarely needed.

**Step-by-step approach:**

1. **History** – differentiate bleeding pattern (bright red, on paper, after stool vs mixed with stool). Ask about alteration in bowel habit, weight loss, abdominal pain, family history of colorectal cancer.
2. **Inspection** – look for external haemorrhoids, skin tags, fissure, fistula, abscess. Ask patient to bear down (Valsalva) – internal haemorrhoids may appear transiently at anal verge.
3. **Digital rectal examination (DRE)** – assess sphincter tone, identify low rectal masses, prostate in males. Internal haemorrhoids are not palpable unless thrombosed or very large. DRE is normal in most Grade I hemorrhoids.
4. **Proctoscopy / anoscopy** – the definitive diagnostic test. Insert proctoscope with patient in left lateral position. Internal haemorrhoids are seen as redundant, purplish cushions at 3, 7, 11 o'clock positions (classic locations). Grade their prolapse by asking patient to strain.
5. **Colonoscopy / sigmoidoscopy** – indicated if **any** alarm feature: age >50, family history of colorectal cancer, black or dark blood mixed with stool, change in bowel habit, weight loss, iron deficiency anaemia, or persistent symptoms despite treatment. "Don't assume it's piles until you've ruled out cancer" – a senior maxim.

**Single finding that confirms:** visible internal haemorrhoid cushion on proctoscopy that bleeds when touched (or spontaneous bleeding is seen).

**Single finding that rules out (for bleeding):** normal proctoscopy/colonoscopy in a patient with bright red blood per rectum. Still possible that bleeding is from a fissure (pain) or from proximal cause (but then blood would be darker). In young patients (<45) with typical story and normal anoscopy, no further workup is needed.

**Labs:** FBC for anaemia if bleeding is heavy or chronic. Coagulation screen only if on anticoagulants. Iron studies if anaemia suspected.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| DRE | Feels normal; may rarely feel a fibrous band or thrombus | Guide further exam; exclude low rectal tumour | Soft non-tender = internal hemorrhoids; hard fixed mass = carcinoma |
| Proctoscopy | Redundant cushions; berry-like; may prolapse | Confirm diagnosis + grade + treatment guide | Bulge >1/3 of lumen = significant; bleeding on contact |
| Colonoscopy | Normal colon and rectum; proximal sources excluded | Rule out malignancy or IBD (if alarm features) | Normal colon → confidently attribute bright red blood to anal source |
| FBC | Hb may be low; MCV may be low if iron deficiency | Quantify chronic blood loss; guide iron replacement | Normocytic = acute; microcytic = chronic iron deficiency |
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – acute pain or bleeding:**
- **If acute thrombosed external haemorrhoid (<72 hours of pain and visible lump):** treat as an emergency. Incision and evacuation of the clot under local anaesthesia (1% lidocaine with adrenaline). Immediately relieves pain. If >72 hours or the pain is already resolving – conservative: analgesia (ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS with food), warm baths, topical lidocaine 5% ointment PRN. No incision after 72 hours (clot is already organised, incision may cause more swelling).
- **If massive bleeding (rare):** resuscitation (IV fluids, crossmatch), urgent proctoscopy to identify source. Topical adrenaline 1:10,000 on a cotton swab can tamponade bleeding. Call senior.
- **If strangulated internal haemorrhoid (irreducible, purple, severe pain):** urgent surgical referral. Do not try to reduce manually (risk of embolism). Analgesia (morphine 5 mg IV if needed), IV fluids, NBM.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – after diagnosis and initial control:**
- **Conservative (base therapy for all):** high-fibre diet (30 g/day), increase fluid intake (2–3 L/day), avoid straining and prolonged sitting. Psyllium husk (1 tbsp once daily) or ispaghula husk (3.5g sachet) titrated to stool consistency. This alone resolves symptoms in ~50% of Grade I–II internal hemorrhoids.
- **Medications:**
  - *Topical analgesia:* lidocaine 5% ointment PRN for external pain – do not exceed 4–6 applications/day.
  - *Topical corticosteroids:* hydrocortisone 2.5% ointment (or suppository 25 mg BD for internal) – short course only (max 7 days). Reduces inflammation and itching. **Pitfall:** prolonged use causes perianal skin atrophy, candida overgrowth.
  - *Oral stool softeners:* lactulose 15 mL BD if constipated, or macrogol (e.g., Movicol) 1–2 sachets daily.
- **Procedure (if symptoms persist ≥1–2 weeks of conservative therapy):** rubber band ligation (RBL) for Grade II–III internal hemorrhoids. The band cuts off blood supply; the cushion sloughs off after ~3–7 days. Success rate >80%. Avoid in patients on anticoagulants (risk of bleeding). Sclerotherapy (5% phenol in almond oil injection) for Grade I–II, especially if on anticoagulants.

**Long-term – preventing recurrence and treating advanced disease:**
- **Lifestyle:** high fibre + adequate fluid lifelong. Avoid chronic straining. Use footstool (knees above hips) during defecation to straighten anorectal angle.
- **If RBL fails or Grade IV prolapse:** surgical haemorrhoidectomy (Milligan-Morgan open, or Ferguson closed). Postoperative pain is severe; requires regular analgesia and stool softeners. Laser or stapled haemorrhoidopexy (PPH) – less pain but higher recurrence.
- **Iron replacement:** if chronic anaemia – oral ferrous sulfate 200 mg TDS for 3 months, then recheck FBC.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do NOT assume bright red per rectum is haemorrhoids in patients >50 without colonoscopy.
- Do NOT prescribe topical steroids for >7 days.
- Do NOT incise a thrombosed external haemorrhoid after 72 hours – causing more harm than benefit.
- Do NOT rubber-band a thrombosed internal hemorrhoid (risk of pelvic sepsis).
- Do NOT try to manually reduce a strangulated internal hemorrhoid on the ward (risk of septic embolisation).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Haemorrhoids | Anal Fissure | Perianal Abscess | Colorectal Cancer |
|---------|--------------|--------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Pain | Usually none (unless thrombosed or strangulated) | Severe sharp pain with defecation, burns for hours after | Constant throbbing pain, worse on sitting, may be minimal if deeper | Usually painless until late; may have vague pressure |
| Bleeding | Bright red, on paper or drips | Bright red, seen on paper, but typically only with stool | No bleeding (unless associated fistula) | Dark or bright red (depending on location) mixed with stool |
| Lump / swelling | External thrombus = blue tender lump; internal prolapse = reducible | Sentinel skin tag (fibrotic oedema) at posterior midline | Erythematous, fluctuant, hot, tender mass; may have purulent discharge (if spontaneous rupture) | Intraluminal mass on DRE; hard, irregular, fixed; may be non-tender |
| Itching | Common due to mucous discharge | Rare | Uncommon (if draining) | Rare |
| Exam findings | Anoscopy: purple cushions; DRE normal | DRE: extreme tenderness; visible tear at 6 or 12 o'clock | DRE: fluctuance, pain; may see exudate | DRE: palpable mass (circumferential or irregular); blood on glove |
| Labs | Normal FBC (unless anaemic) | Normal | Raised CRP, WCC (left shift) | May have anaemia, raised CEA |
| Key distinguishing feature | Painless bleeding + normal DRE + anoscopy reveals cushions | Severe pain with defecation + posterior midline tear on gentle eversion | Fever, toxic look, fluctuant mass – perianal sepsis | Age >50, weight loss, dark blood, family history; mass on DRE |

**Pearl:** *If the patient says “it hurts to wipe,” think fissure. If they say “I see blood when I wipe, but it doesn’t hurt,” think internal haemorrhoids.*
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | Presentation | Key signs | Immediate action |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Thrombosed external haemorrhoid** | Acute severe perianal pain, lump appeared after straining | Tender blue-purple nodule at anal verge <72h | If seen within 72h → incision & evacuation under LA. After 72h → conservative (analgesia, warm baths). |
| **Strangulated internal haemorrhoid** | Severe constant pain, inability to sit, fever, nausea / vomiting | Purple-black prolapsed mass irreducible, tense, sloughing | Urgent surgical consult. IV analgesia, NBM, IV fluids. Do not attempt reduction. |
| **Chronic blood loss anaemia** | Fatigue, pallor, dyspnoea on exertion, palpitations | Microcytic hypochromic anaemia (low Hb, low MCV, low ferritin) | Iron supplementation (oral or IV if severe). Confirm with colonoscopy to exclude proximal source. Rarely, if bleeding massive (haemorrhoidal artery rupture), need surgical haemorrhoidectomy / ligation. |
| **Portal hypertension (anorectal varices)** | Known cirrhosis, heavy painless bleeding | Rectal varices are more tortuous and extend above dentate line (different from haemorrhoids) | Urgent proctoscopy, topical adrenaline, balloon tamponade, TIPS procedure. Bleeding is from portal system – not haemorrhoidal. |
| **Skin tags / fibrosis** | Chronic irritation and discomfort | Redundant perianal skin from resolved thrombus or chronic inflammation | Excision if symptomatic (persistent itching, hygiene issues). Usually cosmetic only. |

**Late-presentation pattern (exam-style stem):** A 55-year-old man with a 3-month history of “piles” self-treated with over-the-counter creams now presents with severe anal pain, a purple irreducible lump, and fever. This is a strangulated internal haemorrhoid with potential progression to perianal sepsis and necrosis – requires emergency surgery.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **GOLF** → Grades of Internal Haemorrhoids:
  - **G**rade I: **B**leed only – no prolapse (B = bleed)
  - **G**rade II: **P**rolapse with straining, **R**educe spontaneously (PR)
  - **G**rade III: **P**rolapse, **M**anually **R**educible (PMR)
  - **G**rade IV: **P**ermanently prolapsed, irreducible (P)
  (Alternatively, a simpler: “I, II, III, IV” – each grade is “bleed only, prolapse+strain+spontaneous, prolapse+manual, permanent”.)

- **BIRD** → Conservative management for Grades I–II:
  - **B**ulk-forming agents (fibre)
  - **I**ncreased fluids
  - **R**est from straining
  - **D**efecation hygiene (clean gently, warm bath)

- **DRE-DSS** → Differential of rectal bleeding (Dysfunction/Diverticulosis, Haemorrhoids, Cancer – but for haemorrhoids specifically, think: “Bleeding: Bright = below, Dark = above”)

**5–7 High-yield clinical pearls:**

1. **“Painless bleeding from pile ≠ pile”** – If the patient also has change in bowel habit, dark blood, weight loss, or a palpable mass on DRE, assume colorectal cancer until proven otherwise.
2. **External haemorrhoids are distal to dentate line → painful.** If a patient has a painful anal lump, it’s either external thrombus or perianal abscess. Differentiate by: abscess has fever, fluctuance, systemic signs; thrombus is a localised blue nodule.
3. **Internal haemorrhoids are insensate → painless.** If a patient with known internal haemorrhoids develops pain, consider: fissure, fistula, abscess, strangulation, or co-existing cancer. Do not dismiss new pain as “just piles”.
4. **Thrombosed external haemorrhoid: the golden window is 72 hours.** If the patient presents within 72 hours of pain onset, incise and evacuate – immediate relief. After 72 hours the clot has organised; incision causes more trauma than benefit.
5. **Rubber band ligation (RBL) is the “gold standard” for Grade II–III internal haemorrhoids.** Success >80%, can be done outpatient. Warn patient: they may have mild discomfort, and a “foreign body” sensation for a few days. Rare but serious complication: pelvic sepsis (fever, urinary retention, severe pain → admit, IV antibiotics, may need thrombectomy).
6. **Always ask about anticoagulation and antiplatelet therapy before RBL.** RBL is contraindicated in patients on warfarin, DOACs, or dual antiplatelets (unless risk-benefit favours and INR/platelets acceptable). Alternative: sclerotherapy.
7. **The dentate line is the “Maginot line” of the anal canal.** Above it = insensate (internal haemorrhoids); below = sensate (external). Mucosal continuity – but nerve supply changes. This explains why you can ligate internal haemorrhoids without local anaesthesia, but you cannot do the same for external.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **“All bright red blood per rectum is haemorrhoids”** – especially in >45 years, always require colonoscopy.
- **Prescribing topical steroids for more than 7 days** – causes skin atrophy, candidal overgrowth, and masks underlying infection.
- **Not performing a DRE on a patient with rectal bleeding** – you miss a low rectal cancer or fissure.
- **Trying to reduce a strangulated internal haemorrhoid manually** – can cause septic embolisation; need emergency surgery.
- **Treating all patients with haemorrhoids as surgical** – most Grade I–II respond to conservative + RBL.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Situation | Criteria | Action |
|-----------|----------|--------|
| **Strangulated internal haemorrhoid** | Irreducible purple/black prolapsed mass, severe constant pain, fever, tachycardia | Urgent surgical referral (registrar or on-call). IV access, analgesia, NBM. |
| **Massive haemorrhoidal bleeding** | Visible arterial bleed on proctoscopy, haemodynamic instability (HR>110, BP<90), NEWS2≥5 | Crossmatch, IV fluids, resuscitation. Call senior surgical registrar. Consider topical adrenaline, tamponade, or emergency haemorrhoidectomy. |
| **Post-RBL

rawMarkdown:
## Haemorrhoids

**One-liner:** Symptomatic anal cushions causing bleeding, prolapse, and discomfort; common in adults, often misdiagnosed as cancer or fissure.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**
A 38-year-old otherwise healthy woman presents with bright red blood on the toilet paper after defecation. She describes occasional anal itching and a sensation of incomplete emptying. She strains to pass hard stools. On direct questioning, she admits to prolonged sitting at work and a low-fibre diet. She is embarrassed and worried it might be cancer. Vital signs are normal. External inspection shows no lesion; digital rectal exam reveals soft, non-tender internal cushions that bulge on straining.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Bright red blood on paper** → low rectal/anal source; dark or mixed blood suggests proximal lesion.
- **Occasional itching** → mucous discharge from prolapsing cushions irritating perianal skin.
- **Sensation of incomplete emptying** → prolapsed haemorrhoids may mimic faecal mass (tenesmus).
- **Straining & hard stools** → exacerbating factors: chronic constipation increases intra-abdominal pressure.
- **Embarrassment and cancer fear** → typical patient delay; need careful reassurance + definitive exam.
- **Normal vitals** → no acute bleeding compromise; anaemia unlikely unless chronic heavy loss.
- **Soft internal cushions on DRE** → internal haemorrhoids above dentate line (insensate) – no pain on palpation.
- **Bulging on straining** → loss of supporting connective tissue; internal haemorrhoids may prolapse with Valsalva.

**Atypical presentations (that may trick you):**
- **Thrombosed external haemorrhoid:** acute severe perianal pain, feels a grape-like lump. The patient may think it's an abscess. No bright red blood, but may have dark discoloration. Urgent.
- **Strangulated internal haemorrhoid:** irreducible prolapse with purple-black discoloration, severe constant pain, possible necrosis. The patient may be febrile and tachycardic – mimics perianal sepsis.
- **Chronic anaemia without visible bleeding:** multiple low-volume bleeds over months. Patient presents with fatigue, pallor, low ferritin. Need to exclude proximal source before attributing to piles – especially if >50 years.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Haemorrhoids are pathological enlargement and distal displacement (prolapse) of the normal anal vascular cushions – not varicose veins. These cushions are composed of arteriovenous sinusoids, smooth muscle (Treitz's muscle), and connective tissue that normally aid fine continence. Disease occurs when the fibromuscular supporting frame degenerates, causing the cushions to slide down the anal canal. Symptoms depend on position: **internal** (above dentate line – insensate, cause painless bleeding/prolapse) vs **external** (below dentate line – sensate, cause pain only if thrombosed).

**Why it matters:**
- **Extremely common:** prevalence ~4–10% in adults; lifetime risk ~50%. Most self-manage or present late.
- **High mimicry profile:** bleeding haemorrhoids must be distinguished from colorectal cancer, inflammatory bowel disease, and anal fissure – especially in patients >45 years or with alarm features.
- **Common pitfalls:** missing external thrombus (patient in agony, misdiagnosed as abscess); assuming all rectal bleeding is 'just piles' (delays cancer diagnosis); over-prescribing topical steroids (skin atrophy, masking infection); under-treating internal prolapse (progression to strangulation).

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**Story of development:**
Anatomy: three main vascular cushions (left lateral, right anterior, right posterior) lie in the submucosa of the anal canal. They are anchored by smooth muscle fibres (Treitz's muscle) and connective tissue that tether them to the internal sphincter and submucosa. This scaffold normally prevents displacement during defecation.

**Triggering events:**
Chronic straining (constipation, prolonged sitting, pregnancy) increases intra-abdominal pressure and shears the anchoring fibres. Over time, the cushions descend axially into the anal canal (Grade I: no prolapse, II: prolapse with straining reducible spontaneously, III: prolapse require manual reduction, IV: permanently prolapsed). As they descend, the overlying mucosa becomes thin and congested. The sinusoids within the cushion become engorged due to loss of external support and subsequent venous stasis.

**How signs arise:**

| Pathophysiologic step | Clinical manifestation | Why / Mechanism |
|----------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Mucosal thinning + faecal abrasion | **Bright red bleeding** | Thin mucosa over engorged sinusoids tears during passage of stool (splatter, on paper, or drips). Bleeding is arterial-sourced (hence bright) because cushions are fed by superior rectal artery branches. |
| Mucous discharge from prolapsed mucosa | **Perianal itching / moisture** | Mucosa secretes mucus; constant wetness causes maceration, irritant dermatitis. |
| Distal displacement of cushions | **Palpable 'lump' on straining** | Loss of fixation allows cushions to slide into the anal canal or externalise. |
| Thrombosis of external haemorrhoid | **Acute painful blue lump** | Straining causes rupture of a vein (subcutaneous haematoma) or in situ thrombosis within an external haemorrhoid. Pain due to stretch of overlying sensate skin (innervated by inferior rectal nerve). |
| Strangulation of prolapsed internal | **Severe constant pain + purple necrosis** | Sphincter spasm traps the prolapsed cushion, impairing venous return → congestion → arterial occlusion → infarction. Pain is severe because ischaemia now affects somatic nerve fibres in the anal verge. |

**Connecting to pattern cues:**
- *Painless bleeding* → internal haemorrhoids above dentate line (visceral afferents only – no pain).
- *Painful lump* → thrombosed external (somatic nerve supply) or strangulated internal.
- *Itching* → mucous seepage. Also consider skin tags (scar from resolved thrombus).
- *Sensation of incomplete emptying* → prolapsing mass creates false rectal fullness.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Specific cause / trigger | Stem clue that points to it |
|----------|--------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Idiopathic (most common)** | Degeneration of supporting connective tissue with age | Patient >40, no other risk factors; typical chronic presentation |
| **Mechanical / lifestyle** | Chronic constipation, straining, prolonged sitting, low-fibre diet | "I push hard to pass small pellets of stool"; office worker; "I eat on the run" |
| **Pregnancy / childbirth** | Increased intra-abdominal pressure + hormonal softening of pelvic ligaments | Complaints started in third trimester or early postpartum |
| **Portal hypertension** | Retrograde flow through portosystemic anastomoses → anorectal varices (not true haemorrhoids but present similarly) | Cirrhosis, ascites, oesophageal varices, splenomegaly; bleeding can be massive |
| **High venous pressure states** | Obesity, chronic cough (COPD), heavy lifting | "I lift weights at the gym" or "I have a smoker's cough" |
| **Malignancy (rare as cause)** | Rectal carcinoma obstructing drainage → secondary haemorrhoids | Unilateral, fixed, hard, or bleeding with altered bowel habit; age >50 |
| **Inflammatory bowel disease** | Crohn's disease (fistulating) or ulcerative colitis | Diarrhoea, abdominal pain, weight loss, extra-intestinal signs |

**Most common cause:** idiopathic degeneration + chronic constipation. Always ask about bowel habit and fibre intake.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnosis is clinical** – no lab test confirms haemorrhoids. Imaging is rarely needed.

**Step-by-step approach:**

1. **History** – differentiate bleeding pattern (bright red, on paper, after stool vs mixed with stool). Ask about alteration in bowel habit, weight loss, abdominal pain, family history of colorectal cancer.
2. **Inspection** – look for external haemorrhoids, skin tags, fissure, fistula, abscess. Ask patient to bear down (Valsalva) – internal haemorrhoids may appear transiently at anal verge.
3. **Digital rectal examination (DRE)** – assess sphincter tone, identify low rectal masses, prostate in males. Internal haemorrhoids are not palpable unless thrombosed or very large. DRE is normal in most Grade I hemorrhoids.
4. **Proctoscopy / anoscopy** – the definitive diagnostic test. Insert proctoscope with patient in left lateral position. Internal haemorrhoids are seen as redundant, purplish cushions at 3, 7, 11 o'clock positions (classic locations). Grade their prolapse by asking patient to strain.
5. **Colonoscopy / sigmoidoscopy** – indicated if **any** alarm feature: age >50, family history of colorectal cancer, black or dark blood mixed with stool, change in bowel habit, weight loss, iron deficiency anaemia, or persistent symptoms despite treatment. "Don't assume it's piles until you've ruled out cancer" – a senior maxim.

**Single finding that confirms:** visible internal haemorrhoid cushion on proctoscopy that bleeds when touched (or spontaneous bleeding is seen).

**Single finding that rules out (for bleeding):** normal proctoscopy/colonoscopy in a patient with bright red blood per rectum. Still possible that bleeding is from a fissure (pain) or from proximal cause (but then blood would be darker). In young patients (<45) with typical story and normal anoscopy, no further workup is needed.

**Labs:** FBC for anaemia if bleeding is heavy or chronic. Coagulation screen only if on anticoagulants. Iron studies if anaemia suspected.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| DRE | Feels normal; may rarely feel a fibrous band or thrombus | Guide further exam; exclude low rectal tumour | Soft non-tender = internal hemorrhoids; hard fixed mass = carcinoma |
| Proctoscopy | Redundant cushions; berry-like; may prolapse | Confirm diagnosis + grade + treatment guide | Bulge >1/3 of lumen = significant; bleeding on contact |
| Colonoscopy | Normal colon and rectum; proximal sources excluded | Rule out malignancy or IBD (if alarm features) | Normal colon → confidently attribute bright red blood to anal source |
| FBC | Hb may be low; MCV may be low if iron deficiency | Quantify chronic blood loss; guide iron replacement | Normocytic = acute; microcytic = chronic iron deficiency |

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – acute pain or bleeding:**
- **If acute thrombosed external haemorrhoid (<72 hours of pain and visible lump):** treat as an emergency. Incision and evacuation of the clot under local anaesthesia (1% lidocaine with adrenaline). Immediately relieves pain. If >72 hours or the pain is already resolving – conservative: analgesia (ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS with food), warm baths, topical lidocaine 5% ointment PRN. No incision after 72 hours (clot is already organised, incision may cause more swelling).
- **If massive bleeding (rare):** resuscitation (IV fluids, crossmatch), urgent proctoscopy to identify source. Topical adrenaline 1:10,000 on a cotton swab can tamponade bleeding. Call senior.
- **If strangulated internal haemorrhoid (irreducible, purple, severe pain):** urgent surgical referral. Do not try to reduce manually (risk of embolism). Analgesia (morphine 5 mg IV if needed), IV fluids, NBM.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – after diagnosis and initial control:**
- **Conservative (base therapy for all):** high-fibre diet (30 g/day), increase fluid intake (2–3 L/day), avoid straining and prolonged sitting. Psyllium husk (1 tbsp once daily) or ispaghula husk (3.5g sachet) titrated to stool consistency. This alone resolves symptoms in ~50% of Grade I–II internal hemorrhoids.
- **Medications:**
  - *Topical analgesia:* lidocaine 5% ointment PRN for external pain – do not exceed 4–6 applications/day.
  - *Topical corticosteroids:* hydrocortisone 2.5% ointment (or suppository 25 mg BD for internal) – short course only (max 7 days). Reduces inflammation and itching. **Pitfall:** prolonged use causes perianal skin atrophy, candida overgrowth.
  - *Oral stool softeners:* lactulose 15 mL BD if constipated, or macrogol (e.g., Movicol) 1–2 sachets daily.
- **Procedure (if symptoms persist ≥1–2 weeks of conservative therapy):** rubber band ligation (RBL) for Grade II–III internal hemorrhoids. The band cuts off blood supply; the cushion sloughs off after ~3–7 days. Success rate >80%. Avoid in patients on anticoagulants (risk of bleeding). Sclerotherapy (5% phenol in almond oil injection) for Grade I–II, especially if on anticoagulants.

**Long-term – preventing recurrence and treating advanced disease:**
- **Lifestyle:** high fibre + adequate fluid lifelong. Avoid chronic straining. Use footstool (knees above hips) during defecation to straighten anorectal angle.
- **If RBL fails or Grade IV prolapse:** surgical haemorrhoidectomy (Milligan-Morgan open, or Ferguson closed). Postoperative pain is severe; requires regular analgesia and stool softeners. Laser or stapled haemorrhoidopexy (PPH) – less pain but higher recurrence.
- **Iron replacement:** if chronic anaemia – oral ferrous sulfate 200 mg TDS for 3 months, then recheck FBC.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do NOT assume bright red per rectum is haemorrhoids in patients >50 without colonoscopy.
- Do NOT prescribe topical steroids for >7 days.
- Do NOT incise a thrombosed external haemorrhoid after 72 hours – causing more harm than benefit.
- Do NOT rubber-band a thrombosed internal hemorrhoid (risk of pelvic sepsis).
- Do NOT try to manually reduce a strangulated internal hemorrhoid on the ward (risk of septic embolisation).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Haemorrhoids | Anal Fissure | Perianal Abscess | Colorectal Cancer |
|---------|--------------|--------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Pain | Usually none (unless thrombosed or strangulated) | Severe sharp pain with defecation, burns for hours after | Constant throbbing pain, worse on sitting, may be minimal if deeper | Usually painless until late; may have vague pressure |
| Bleeding | Bright red, on paper or drips | Bright red, seen on paper, but typically only with stool | No bleeding (unless associated fistula) | Dark or bright red (depending on location) mixed with stool |
| Lump / swelling | External thrombus = blue tender lump; internal prolapse = reducible | Sentinel skin tag (fibrotic oedema) at posterior midline | Erythematous, fluctuant, hot, tender mass; may have purulent discharge (if spontaneous rupture) | Intraluminal mass on DRE; hard, irregular, fixed; may be non-tender |
| Itching | Common due to mucous discharge | Rare | Uncommon (if draining) | Rare |
| Exam findings | Anoscopy: purple cushions; DRE normal | DRE: extreme tenderness; visible tear at 6 or 12 o'clock | DRE: fluctuance, pain; may see exudate | DRE: palpable mass (circumferential or irregular); blood on glove |
| Labs | Normal FBC (unless anaemic) | Normal | Raised CRP, WCC (left shift) | May have anaemia, raised CEA |
| Key distinguishing feature | Painless bleeding + normal DRE + anoscopy reveals cushions | Severe pain with defecation + posterior midline tear on gentle eversion | Fever, toxic look, fluctuant mass – perianal sepsis | Age >50, weight loss, dark blood, family history; mass on DRE |

**Pearl:** *If the patient says “it hurts to wipe,” think fissure. If they say “I see blood when I wipe, but it doesn’t hurt,” think internal haemorrhoids.*

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | Presentation | Key signs | Immediate action |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Thrombosed external haemorrhoid** | Acute severe perianal pain, lump appeared after straining | Tender blue-purple nodule at anal verge <72h | If seen within 72h → incision & evacuation under LA. After 72h → conservative (analgesia, warm baths). |
| **Strangulated internal haemorrhoid** | Severe constant pain, inability to sit, fever, nausea / vomiting | Purple-black prolapsed mass irreducible, tense, sloughing | Urgent surgical consult. IV analgesia, NBM, IV fluids. Do not attempt reduction. |
| **Chronic blood loss anaemia** | Fatigue, pallor, dyspnoea on exertion, palpitations | Microcytic hypochromic anaemia (low Hb, low MCV, low ferritin) | Iron supplementation (oral or IV if severe). Confirm with colonoscopy to exclude proximal source. Rarely, if bleeding massive (haemorrhoidal artery rupture), need surgical haemorrhoidectomy / ligation. |
| **Portal hypertension (anorectal varices)** | Known cirrhosis, heavy painless bleeding | Rectal varices are more tortuous and extend above dentate line (different from haemorrhoids) | Urgent proctoscopy, topical adrenaline, balloon tamponade, TIPS procedure. Bleeding is from portal system – not haemorrhoidal. |
| **Skin tags / fibrosis** | Chronic irritation and discomfort | Redundant perianal skin from resolved thrombus or chronic inflammation | Excision if symptomatic (persistent itching, hygiene issues). Usually cosmetic only. |

**Late-presentation pattern (exam-style stem):** A 55-year-old man with a 3-month history of “piles” self-treated with over-the-counter creams now presents with severe anal pain, a purple irreducible lump, and fever. This is a strangulated internal haemorrhoid with potential progression to perianal sepsis and necrosis – requires emergency surgery.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **GOLF** → Grades of Internal Haemorrhoids:
  - **G**rade I: **B**leed only – no prolapse (B = bleed)
  - **G**rade II: **P**rolapse with straining, **R**educe spontaneously (PR)
  - **G**rade III: **P**rolapse, **M**anually **R**educible (PMR)
  - **G**rade IV: **P**ermanently prolapsed, irreducible (P)
  (Alternatively, a simpler: “I, II, III, IV” – each grade is “bleed only, prolapse+strain+spontaneous, prolapse+manual, permanent”.)

- **BIRD** → Conservative management for Grades I–II:
  - **B**ulk-forming agents (fibre)
  - **I**ncreased fluids
  - **R**est from straining
  - **D**efecation hygiene (clean gently, warm bath)

- **DRE-DSS** → Differential of rectal bleeding (Dysfunction/Diverticulosis, Haemorrhoids, Cancer – but for haemorrhoids specifically, think: “Bleeding: Bright = below, Dark = above”)

**5–7 High-yield clinical pearls:**

1. **“Painless bleeding from pile ≠ pile”** – If the patient also has change in bowel habit, dark blood, weight loss, or a palpable mass on DRE, assume colorectal cancer until proven otherwise.
2. **External haemorrhoids are distal to dentate line → painful.** If a patient has a painful anal lump, it’s either external thrombus or perianal abscess. Differentiate by: abscess has fever, fluctuance, systemic signs; thrombus is a localised blue nodule.
3. **Internal haemorrhoids are insensate → painless.** If a patient with known internal haemorrhoids develops pain, consider: fissure, fistula, abscess, strangulation, or co-existing cancer. Do not dismiss new pain as “just piles”.
4. **Thrombosed external haemorrhoid: the golden window is 72 hours.** If the patient presents within 72 hours of pain onset, incise and evacuate – immediate relief. After 72 hours the clot has organised; incision causes more trauma than benefit.
5. **Rubber band ligation (RBL) is the “gold standard” for Grade II–III internal haemorrhoids.** Success >80%, can be done outpatient. Warn patient: they may have mild discomfort, and a “foreign body” sensation for a few days. Rare but serious complication: pelvic sepsis (fever, urinary retention, severe pain → admit, IV antibiotics, may need thrombectomy).
6. **Always ask about anticoagulation and antiplatelet therapy before RBL.** RBL is contraindicated in patients on warfarin, DOACs, or dual antiplatelets (unless risk-benefit favours and INR/platelets acceptable). Alternative: sclerotherapy.
7. **The dentate line is the “Maginot line” of the anal canal.** Above it = insensate (internal haemorrhoids); below = sensate (external). Mucosal continuity – but nerve supply changes. This explains why you can ligate internal haemorrhoids without local anaesthesia, but you cannot do the same for external.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **“All bright red blood per rectum is haemorrhoids”** – especially in >45 years, always require colonoscopy.
- **Prescribing topical steroids for more than 7 days** – causes skin atrophy, candidal overgrowth, and masks underlying infection.
- **Not performing a DRE on a patient with rectal bleeding** – you miss a low rectal cancer or fissure.
- **Trying to reduce a strangulated internal haemorrhoid manually** – can cause septic embolisation; need emergency surgery.
- **Treating all patients with haemorrhoids as surgical** – most Grade I–II respond to conservative + RBL.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| Situation | Criteria | Action |
|-----------|----------|--------|
| **Strangulated internal haemorrhoid** | Irreducible purple/black prolapsed mass, severe constant pain, fever, tachycardia | Urgent surgical referral (registrar or on-call). IV access, analgesia, NBM. |
| **Massive haemorrhoidal bleeding** | Visible arterial bleed on proctoscopy, haemodynamic instability (HR>110, BP<90), NEWS2≥5 | Crossmatch, IV fluids, resuscitation. Call senior surgical registrar. Consider topical adrenaline, tamponade, or emergency haemorrhoidectomy. |
| **Post-RBL
